title: Open Kitchen Shelving with Rustic Brackets
summary: Wall-hung open shelving on mitered 1x2 brackets, sized to hold double 1x6 or single 1x10/1x12 shelf boards.
description:
The bracket is a small triangle of three pieces — a vertical back leg, a horizontal top, and a mitered diagonal cross support — but that cross support carries real weight, so its placement and fastening deserve more care than the size suggests. Glue the cross support in and let it cure fully before nailing through the back into it; nailing a glue joint before it's set just pushes the parts out of alignment. Nail closer to the inside edge of the cross support rather than the center, since that's where the wood is thickest and least likely to split.

This is genuinely load-bearing kitchen storage, not decoration, which is why the brackets have to land on wall studs — not drywall anchors. Predrilling the mounting holes in the brackets before you try to drive screws through them and into the wall makes this step far more manageable, especially reaching behind the cross support where a drill has limited clearance.

tools.note: For the 45° cross-support cuts on each bracket.

materials.name: Pine, 1x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Brackets — one set of three pieces per bracket.

materials.name: Pine, 1x10 or 1x12, 8 ft
materials.note: Or double up 1x6 boards for the shelving instead.

materials.name: Wood screws, wall-mounting
materials.note: Long enough to reach solidly into wall studs.

cutList.note: Per bracket — build one set of three pieces for each bracket needed.
Long-point to long-point, ends cut at 45° perpendicular to each other.
Cut to fit your own wall span; can be 1x10, 1x12, or doubled 1x6 boards.

steps.title: Build the bracket frame
steps.body: Attach the bracket's bottom leg to the top piece on the back side, using glue and a 1-1/4" pocket screw.

steps.title: Add the cross support
steps.body: Clamp the bracket securely to your workbench and mark the exact placement of the cross support — this matters, since it's what carries the shelf's load. Glue the cross support in and let it dry fully before nailing through the back into it. Nail closer to the inside edge of the cross support rather than the center, where the wood is thickest and least likely to split.

steps.title: Attach shelving and mount to the wall
steps.body: Attach the shelf boards to the finished brackets with glue and screws, nails, or staples. Paint or stain the whole assembly now, before it's on the wall. Predrill mounting holes in the brackets, then secure the brackets to wall studs — not drywall alone. Reaching behind the cross support to drive a screw is a little tricky, but a screwdriver bit or a stubby drill will get you there.

images.alt: Rustic open kitchen shelving mounted on mitered wooden brackets
